coredata.dc:title: Controlled synthesis of blue luminescent graphene quantum dots from carbonized citric acid: Assessment of methodology, stability, and fluorescence in an aqueous environment
coredata.dc:creator: More, Mahesh P.
Lohar, Pravinkumar H.
Patil, Ashwini G.
Patil, Pravin O.
Deshmukh, Prashant K.
coredata.dc:description: The present investigation deals with a comparative assessment of various techniques for the synthesis of blue luminescence Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) using various equipments like furnace, domestic microwave synthesiser and scientific microwave synthesiser using citric acid as a precursor. A bottom–up method was adapted to develop photoluminescent (PL) GQDs and assessed for luminescence intensity of GQDs at different environmental conditions. The methodology requires very less concentration of NaOH to disperse GQDs. The present approach is advantageous over other conventional organic solvent mediated synthesis, as it requires less time, easy to reproduce and disperse in water, furthermore it produces stable fluorescence for a longer period of time at ambient temperature conditions. The synthesized GQDs are primarily characterized by UV for detection of the fluorescence intensity and simultaneously Ultraviolet–Visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy and Fourier Transform Infra Red (FTIR) Spectroscopy to assess the up conversion from the precursor molecule. Apart from these techniques, Particle Size and Zeta Potential, Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), Elemental Analysis (EDX), Raman Spectroscopy and Fluorescence spectrophotometry were used to characterise synthesized GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Scientific microwave
Blue luminescence
Aqueous synthesis
Carbonization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Particle size and zeta potential analysis
Selection and optimization of synthesis strategy
Concentration and volume of the dispersion medium

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesized GQDs excited in UV cabinet a) Visible Light, b) Short wavelength (254 nm), c) Long wavelength (365 nm).
GQD synthesized by using a) furnace, b) Conventional Microwave, c) CEM scientific microwave.
PL intensity based on concentration of Citric Acid used during the study.
PL intensity of Variable Irradiation time for synthesis of GQD in CEM scientific microwave.
UV spectroscopic analysis of variable irradiation time to synthesize GQDs.
PL intensity for GQDs dispersed in a) aq. NaOH, b) Dist. Water, c) Phosphate Buffer pH 7.4, d) Borate Buffer pH 8.6.
UV Spectrum of GQD dispersed in different solvents.
GQDs dispersed in a different volume of solvent a) UV Spectrum b) PL intensity.
GQD dispersed in a variable volume of solvent a) PL intensity, b) UV Spectrum.
PL Intensity for GQD dispersion heated at a different temperature.

Graph of fluorescence spectroscopy of GQD in different pH condition, 1) B4 S-G QD in basic pH after 48 h 2) C3 - GQD in acidic pH, 3) D3 SGQD in Neutral pH after 15 days, 4) C3 S - GQD in acidic pH after 48 h (5) D3 – GQD in Neutral pH, (6) B4 -GQD in basic pH.
SEM Image of GQDs (a & b), c) Elemental analysis of GQDs.
Particle size and Zeta potential of optimized GQDs.
Variable Irradiation Time used for synthesis of GQDs.
Different types of Dispersion Medium for GQDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: **Note: Bold row shows optimized batch used for further characterization and verification using analytical methods.
**Note: Bold row shows optimized batch used for further characterization and verification using analytical methods.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Change in volume of NaOH solution.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: **Note: Bold row shows optimized batch used for further characterization and verification using analytical methods.

head.ce:title: Controlled synthesis of blue luminescent graphene quantum dots from carbonized citric acid: Assessment of methodology, stability, and fluorescence in an aqueous environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Post Graduate Department of Pharmaceutics, H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Karvand Naka, Shirpur, Dist - Dhule, 425405, M.S, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Karvand Naka, Shirpur, Dist - Dhule, 425405, M.S, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microbiology and Biotechnology, R.C. Patel Arts, Commerce and Science College, Karwand Naka, Shirpur, Dist - Dhule, 425405, M.S, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present investigation deals with a comparative assessment of various techniques for the synthesis of blue luminescence Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) using various equipments like furnace, domestic microwave synthesiser and scientific microwave synthesiser using citric acid as a precursor. A bottom–up method was adapted to develop photoluminescent (PL) GQDs and assessed for luminescence intensity of GQDs at different environmental conditions. The methodology requires very less concentration of NaOH to disperse GQDs. The present approach is advantageous over other conventional organic solvent mediated synthesis, as it requires less time, easy to reproduce and disperse in water, furthermore it produces stable fluorescence for a longer period of time at ambient temperature conditions. The synthesized GQDs are primarily characterized by UV for detection of the fluorescence intensity and simultaneously Ultraviolet–Visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy and Fourier Transform Infra Red (FTIR) Spectroscopy to assess the up conversion from the precursor molecule. Apart from these techniques, Particle Size and Zeta Potential, Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), Elemental Analysis (EDX), Raman Spectroscopy and Fluorescence spectrophotometry were used to characterise synthesized GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: From last few decades when the nanotechnology starts exploring at the edge; becoming a new area that represents small sized materials, structures, devices, and systems. Nanometer scale size ranging between 1 and 100 nm is considered the most promising application in nanomedicine and other technical approaches [1]. Novel technical aspects can be possible with help of Nanomaterials to produce an efficient system with wide range of applications such as drug delivery systems; performance based medical devices, diagnostic materials, etc. [2,3].

The demand of nanomaterials has increased in recent years, due to their unique properties and structural features. The application area is going to increase day by day with varying its phases or in different types of areas such as catalysis, biomedical, drug delivery and many more areas are still exploiting. Few of these materials includes the carbon-based luminescent nanomaterials (CLNMs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [4], nanodiamonds [5], Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) fragment and surface functionalized CNTs [6,7], Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to name a few, are exploring more due to low toxicity, high luminescence, robust material, chemically inertness and ease for synthesis [8].

Graphene is a zero band gap semiconductor with infinite exciton Bohr diameter. GQDs can be differentiated from graphene as having one or few layered graphene sheets with high electron transport in 3 spatial dimension, which is basically due to small fragments available [9–11]. These small confinements can also useful to denote the size of GQDs which basically within 20 nm. The experimental and theoretical scientists are working to break out the challenges behind synthesis of GQDs and structural variability between graphene and GQDs. Till date, the top down approaches have been used to fabricate the GQDs by means of cutting or fragmentation of graphene sheets, it includes Hydrothermal Synthesis [12], Amino hydrothermal synthesis [13], Solvothermal synthesis [14], Microwave assisted synthesis [15], Microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis [16], Ultrasonic chemistry synthesis [17], Acidic Oxidation synthesis [18], etc. On the other hand, organic chemists have modified it by utilizing bottom-up approaches with well defined molecular structure [19,20] consist of precursor pyrolysis synthesis [21], Pyrolysis and exfoliation [22], etc.

Liquid Phase reactions were preferably used to synthesize GQDs using aromatic compounds, in recent years solid phase reactions are carried out to increase the product and quantum yield. Citric acid, ascorbic acid, sugar, glucose, sucrose, etc. were used as precursor in solid phase reactions; whereas furnace or microwave assisted hydrothermal methods are recently emerging. The mechanism of GQDs formation was postulated by many researchers, in microwave assisted hydrothermal route citric acid (CA) prone to decomposition at melting temperature with subsequent formation of hydronium ion which acts as catalyst for self assembly forming nanocrystalline GQDs. The polymerization and subsequent condensation pertains intermolecular hydrolysis reaction started from hydronium ion [23,24]. Naik J. P. et al. proposed GQDs was formed from concentric aromatic clusters and super saturation after burst nucleation through aldol condensation and cycloaddition reactions [25].

GQDs synthesized via both approaches i.e. by means of top-down and bottom-up have near to similar characteristic features, they can show photoluminescence (PL) behaviour with different colours such as deep ultraviolet, blue, green, yellow and red. Several mechanisms were proposed to show the luminescence effect, but it is basically due to intrinsic defective emission state arising from small fragments of graphene sheets. The luminescence induced might be due to either quantum size effect, zigzag edges of sheet or recombination of the localized electron-hole pair [26]. Optoelectronic properties of GQDs can be tuned by controlling the chemical functionalities, by varying its synthesis environment and surface doping by simple means of changing pH conditions from neutral, acidic or basic. The dispersion medium or pH of the solution helps to enhance the conductivity as well as ion donating ability due to presence of free charged ions [27].

Exploring the biomedical application of graphene and its derivative, some of the studies revealed that, graphene and its derivative are biocompatible and non cytotoxic to biological tissue. Many advantages and applications were explored and well documented in the recent literature [28,29].

In the present investigation, an attempt has been made to synthesize blue luminescent GQDs using citric acid as a precursor molecule in furnace, domestic microwave synthesiser and scientific microwave synthesiser at controlled temperature and pressure. This bottom-up method was consisted of varying the carbonization degree of precursor molecule to enhance the luminescence effect. Effect of dispersing agents and concentration of dispersed phase on PL intensity and stability of GQDs was studied using NaOH, phosphate, borate, and water. Effect of temperature on PL intensity of GQDs were also studied which gave promising results. The Quantum yield of as synthesized GQDs was calculated from quinine sulphate as standard and found to be 6.91%. The synthesized and stabilized GQDs can be used for biomedical applications with variation range such as sensing, luminescent effect, bioimaging, etc. The aim of present study was to develop an optimized and reproducible method for effective GQD synthesis.

The citric acid and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Rankem Chemicals Pvt. Ltd. Mumbai, India. The Boric acid and potassium chloride were procured from Merck Specialities Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India. All other chemicals and reagents used for the study were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

The method used for synthesis and optimization of stable photoluminescence GQDs involves, microwave irradiation at controlled temperature and pressure (CEM Microwave, USA). An accurately weighed quantity of citric acid (2 g) was transferred into a Teflon-sealed tube and irradiated. The optimized parameters were set for the batch as mentioned in Table 1 with reaction temperature, which was kept constant at 180 °C. The optimization was performed by checking visual luminescence intensity (in UV cabinet) and UV–vis spectra for each sample at a certain time interval at room temperature. These input parameters were set in the software as, the temperature was set at 180 °C, Power 150 Watt, and pressure was controlled to 10 psi, for 10 min holding time and 10 min run time. After Irradiation samples were cooled down to room temperature and added to the dispersion medium.

Physicochemical parameters were assessed to check the stability of GQDs in a different environment. Stability of synthesized GQDs was observed by optical properties measuring luminescence behaviour in UV cabinet which was further confirmed by measuring UV–vis spectra at 230 nm. The study was designed for assessment of GQDs stability by changing the irradiation time, type of dispersion medium, concentration, and volume of dispersion. These selected parameters could indirectly affected by stability and degree of carbonization of GQDs.

Various reports suggest different method could be adapted to synthesize GQDs, but in this investigation, we have made an attempt to develop a robust, simple and reproducible method using the scientific microwave, which can easily control process parameters and gives an efficient, effective with high luminescence and stable GQDs. Selection of method is a primary concern which helps to reduce the time required for optimization and quantity of raw material used. For a selection of best suited method, we have chosen various equipments for processing; such as furnace, conventional microwave, and CEM microwave synthesiser for proper comparison. During processing, concentration of citric acid was kept constant and process parameters were changed and set according to reported literature. During selection, an equal amount of citric acid was taken for the study and the luminescence intensity was observed using UV cabinet. The batch with highest luminescent and stable PL produced from these methods was characterized further.

For the synthesis of GQDs using furnace assisted method, a weighed quantity of 2.00 g of citric acid was placed in silica crucible and heated at 180 °C for 25 min. When it turns in orange coloured semisolid viscous solution, it was transferred into 100 mL aq. NaOH solution 10 mg/mL concentration and stirred at 100 rpm at room temperature.

For the synthesis of GQDs by domestic microwave assisted method, a weighed quantity of 2.00 g of citric acid was added in a beaker and irradiated at power of 150 Watt for 90 min. The sample was irradiated for certain period of time till it became an orange coloured semisolid viscous solution. This solution is then transferred into 100 mL aq. NaOH solution 10 mg/mL concentration and stirred at 100 rpm at room temperature.

The scientific microwave method was found to be best suited method for synthesis of stable GQDs compared to furnace and domestic microwave oven. After selection of method optimization of irradiation time was the one important parameter to assess. As the time increases the heating effect was also raised and rate of conversion was fluctuated. Effect of gradual increase in irradiation time is reported in Table 1, which substantially represent the stability of GQDs at different time intervals. Formation of GQDs is confirmed by visual analysis of luminescence intensity in UV cabinet at short and long wavelength by measuring absorbance at 230 nm.

Optimized batch of GQDs was analyzed by using UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (UV 1800 Shimadzu, Japan). Measurement performed against blank of respective dispersion medium used for dispersing GQDs. Samples were scanned between 200 and 400 nm and absorption maxima was noted at λ max 230 nm.

The FT-IR spectrum was recorded for optimized GQD batch; % transmittance peak from the spectra was measured and recorded using FTIR spectrophotometer (IR-Affinity-1, Shimadzu, Japan) with diffuse reluctant substance (DRS) technique. The samples were mixed with potassium bromide (KBr) of IR grade in the ratio of 1:100. The obtained results were compared with reported functional groups and the formation of GQDs after carbonization of citric acid was confirmed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size and zeta potential analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Particle size and zeta potential plays a crucial role in synthesis and optimization of nanomaterials. Particle size can measure the hydrodynamic diameter of prepared nanomaterial whereas zeta potential measured the surface charge and its stability in solution. An optimized batch of GQDs was analyzed for particle size and zeta potential measurement using Dynamic Light Scattering NanoPlus 3 (Particulate System, Micromeritics, USA). The sample was diluted to identify the recommended scattering intensity and diluted further with water. Measurements were done in triplicate at room temperature and standard protocol was followed as provided by the instrument manufacturer.

The surface morphology and characteristics of carbonized citric acid were studied by Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) (Hitachi S-4800). The use of Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDS or EDX) detects the elements present in the selected area of FESEM image providing qualitative and quantitative surface analysis. Before analysis, the GQDs sample was prepared by placing droplet onto aluminum stub containing carbon paper, dried overnight and sputtered using gold prior to imaging and analysis at 20 kV accelerating voltage.

Raman analysis is a promising and widely used analytical method for confirmation of carbon-based nanomaterials. Carbon–carbon double bond interfacial intensity would be high in Raman spectrum. Raman analysis of optimizing sample of GQDs was done using RENISHAW, InVia Raman spectrometer with a 514 nm laser at a power of 4.7 mW. Instrument specific parameters were set at room temperature and the sample was placed in glass cuvette and scanned in between 1000 and 2500 nm with 1800 grating.

Fluorescence intensity of synthesized GQDs was characterized by using Fluorimetry (FLUOROMAX-4 HORIBA Jobin Yvon, USA). The synthesized GQDs were dispersed in different solution of varying pH values such as 5.6, 12.6 and 7.0 as the final pH of the solution. It was studied by placing in a quartz cuvette and excited at 365 nm at 25 °C. Intensity was measured and compared with the reported results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection and optimization of synthesis strategy
ce:section.ce:para: A selection of proper synthesis method can reduce the time required for process optimization which substantially reduces the efforts as well as may be an economical alternative to cut down in process cost. Development of more robust and reproducible methods is necessity of time for outgrowth of GQDs for biomedical applications. Hence, before starting with proper method for optimization of GQDs, we have assessed the degree of carbonization of citric acid.

On a preliminary basis, we evaluated the carbonized citric acid in UV cabinet at a different wavelength. Changes from visible light, short wavelength (254 nm) to long wavelength (365 nm) were monitored. Carbonized citric acid from microwave irradiation forms a viscous solution, which was dispersed in a suitable dispersion medium, observed in UV cabinet demonstrated a wide variety of luminance produced at their respective excitation wavelength. Fig. 1 , shows behaviour of synthesized carbonized material at different wavelengths.

The dispersed solution at visible light (Fig. 1a) appears weak pale yellow in color, while at short excitation UV wavelength 254 nm and a long excitation wavelength of 365 nm shows green and blue fluorescence, respectively. Color excitation was readily observed by naked eyes only. The variation in color/fluorescence from the prepared carbonized citric acid is might be due to solvent attachment or different emissive traps on the surface of nanomaterials. This carbonized material was further characterized to confirm, whether the synthesized nanomaterial is specifically a GQDs or QDs or carbon nanostructures, etc.

A set of experiments was carried out as reported in previous literature, and methods were compared for easiness, and time required for the reaction with highest luminescence intensity. As described in methodology section, citric acid was carbonized using three types of equipments (air furnace, conventional microwave, and scientific microwave) at specified reaction conditions, as mentioned in method section and results were comparatively assessed. The prepared nanomaterial showed blue luminescence produced at long wavelength. Fig. 2 , represents the intensity produced from irradiated samples of carbonized citric acid using three different instrumental methods. From Fig. 2 c), it is evident that fluorescence intensity is highest in case of scientific microwave synthesiser, compared to GQDs produce by conventional microwave and furnace. One more advantage observed during the study is that, the time required for optimum carbonization is very less in scientific microwave wave than other methods. The reaction was completed within 8 min in the scientific microwave, while furnace and domestic microwave required 25–90 min to carbonize the citric acid. Hence, for further optimization and stability studies, scientific microwave assisted method was selected and examined.

Very few studies have been reported in the literature regarding the stability of fluorescence intensity of GQDs suspended in different media. Upon storage at different physical conditions, fluorescence property of GQDs may vary with different dependent and independent variables. It might be due to the concentration of GQDs, type and pH of media used for the GQDs suspension, storage condition, etc.

In this study, we have recorded the alterations in fluorescence intensity without changing size and concentration of GQDs. The effect of light and temperature on fluorescence intensity upon storage of GQDs was checked by suspending these GQDs in an aqueous NaOH solution.

After selection of a proper method for synthesis of GQDs, precursor concentration was ascertained as it plays an important role in synthesis and stability. We have verified it by using a different concentration of citric acid carbonized in the scientific microwave. Variable concentration, ranging from 1 to 2.5 g of citric acid was used during the study; the other reaction parameters were set to be constant. From Fig. 3 , it is evident that the microwave irradiated 2 g concentration of citric acid shows highest fluorescence intensity. The carbonized material was dispersed in a dispersion medium and observed under UV cabinet at a longer wavelength. Even though, concentration of 2.5 g shows similar fluorescence intensity, it was disappeared after 24 h of storage at room temperature in the dark area. Hence, we have chosen and finalized the concentration of 2.0 g citric acid as an optimum concentration for further studies.

The irradiation time is an important parameter after selection of method; it is helpful for verification of degree of carbonization of citric acid used to convert into GQDs. Irradiation time was increased for 4, 6, 8 10, 12, 14 and 16 min (Fig. 4 ) to check the carbonization of citric acid converted to GQDs with stable fluorescence. While other parameters are set to be constant at each variable such as concentration of citric acid, pressure, temperature, and irradiation power. From Fig. 4, it is clear that wide range of variability of fluorescence may be produced after variability in interval of time. Irradiation time was fixed to 8 min as an optimum, because it has stable fluorescence up to 8 days in aqueous NaOH solution. While incremental time above 8 min was found to be unstable and loses its intensity after 24 h. At 10–16 min of irradiation, the color turns into brownish black. The change in darker side represents somewhat extra degree of carbonization. In fact, the phenomenon suggests its conversion into graphene oxide instead of GQDs.

The study was verified using UV–Vis spectroscopic analysis. As shown in Fig. 5 , more intense peak present at 230 nm corresponds to GQDs. Gradual increase in time by 2 min i.e. from 4 to 8 min shows an increase in absorbance intensity. While at 8 min, it shows the highest absorbance. Whereas after 8 min of irradiation time, each 2 min increment shows a decrease in absorption intensity up to 16 min which has least absorbance at 230 nm. This might be due to the free zigzag sites from GQDs, which are protonated at the acidic condition, which are trying to accumulate and forms a reversible complex between free zigzag sites and H+ ions available in solution. Before leading to form an emissive carbene triple state it gets broken down and become fluorescence inactive after reversible complex formation. In alkaline conditions, the free zigzag sites are restored, thereby leading to the restoration of fluorescence [30,31].

The role of the dispersing agent is important in stabilizing the GQDs in a solution state. During the study, we have selected Distilled Water (DW), Phosphate buffer pH 7.4, Borate buffer pH 8.6 and aqueous NaOH solutions with constant volumes. It represents the equal distribution of GQDs among the solution. The GQDs were well dispersed in all solutions selected for study; it shows pale yellow colour in a visible light, while blue fluorescence produced by excitation at 365 nm in UV cabinet.

Fluorescence intensity was compared with individual samples as described above. From Fig. 6 , it is evident that the fluorescence intensity of GQDs dispersed in aqueous NaOH showed the highest luminescence at wavelength 365 nm. Color difference can be easily seen visually, as other buffer solutions containing PL intensity disappeared after 24 h storage at room temperature in a dark environment. Some observations are made in terms of borate buffer pH 8.6, like it shows immediate quenching of fluorescence. We carried forward aqueous NaOH solution for further studies, as it gives slightly acidic pH values near to 6.5 and shows stability for a week at room temperature in dark environment.

The UV–Visible Spectroscopy Analysis was utilized for synthesized GQD dispersed in aqueous NaOH solution, distilled water, phosphate buffer pH 7.4 and borate buffer pH 8.6 and analyzed by UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Fig. 7 ). An absorbance maxima near 230 nm were observed in all dispersing solvent, while aqueous NaOH shows highest absorption intensity compare with other selected dispersion solvents. The relative study compared with fluorescence intensity produced at a larger wavelength from UV cabinet shows stable and reproducible GQDs. aqueous NaOH solution was used for further studies to disperse GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration and volume of the dispersion medium
ce:section.ce:para: By selecting the appropriate dispersion medium, we have designed stability protocol in an aqueous NaOH solution and further proceed for changing the concentration and volume of aq. NaOH solution. As described earlier in methodology, different concentration ranging from 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 mg/mL were used for dispersing GQDs with equal volume. Fig. 8 predicts the highest intensity which was observed in 10 mg/mL concentrated aqueous NaOH solution, and it lasts for more than 8 days, if stored in the dark environment. The 15, 20 and 25 mg/mL concentrated aqueous NaOH also has highest intensity but was disappeared upon storage for a longer period of time and pH of the solution goes on the more basic side that will limit its use in biomedical applications. 5 mg/mL solution does not show any type of PL intensity as it is quenched due to lower concentration to expand its epitaxial sites.

Similarly, UV spectrum from Fig. 8 shows decrease absorption maxima as the concentration of NaOH as dispersion medium increases from 5 to 25 mg/mL. The 5 mg/mL concentrated dispersion shows the highest absorbance at 230 nm but losses PL intensity at a longer wavelength, while 10 mg/mL concentrate has less acidic pH and shows highest and stable luminescence intensity. As per the observation 10 mg/mL is an appropriate concentration for dispersion medium which shows maximum intensity with stability for a longer period of time. After selecting the appropriate concentration, the volume of dispersion also plays important role in stabilizing the GQDs at aqueous interfaces. By picking up 10 mg/mL of concentrated aq. NaOH dispersion, volume differentiating from 10, 25, 50, 75, 100 mL was used for the study.

Fluorescence intensity was compared to each individual volume of the dispersion medium. From Fig. 9 a, it is clear that the highest fluorescence intensity was present at 100 mL volume concentrate, which is likely to be decreased as the volume decreases simultaneously. UV spectroscopic observations are shown in Fig. 9b; the absorption maxima for 100 mL volume of dispersion medium was high, while correspondingly decreases with decreasing volume of dispersion medium at an equivalent concentration.

Quenching of fluorescence intensity was affected by temperature. Verifiable results were obtained after GQDs dispersion heated at different temperatures ranging from 40, 60 and 80 °C for 1 h. Results obtained after heating the GQDs dispersion are depicted in Fig. 10 . The dispersion heated at 40 °C for 1 h does not show any change in fluorescence intensity while at an incremental temperature of 60 and 80 °C, shows a reduction in fluorescence intensity at a longer wavelength in UV cabinet [32].

The final optimized GQD dispersions were analyzed for fluorescence intensity using spectrophotometer from 420 to 470 nm emission wavelength. Stability was assessed for individual samples at variable pH (pH 5.6, 7.0 and 12.6) assess up to 48 h at room temperature. Excitation maxima were compared with the variable pH and stability of same sample, after 48 h an emission spectra of GQDs was observed at 365 nm, which is the highest excitation maxima noted and compared with the reported literature [31].

Fig. 11 depicts, neutral pH shows highest fluorescence maxima compared to pH 5.6 and pH 12.6. The GQDs dispersed in highly basic pH i.e. 12.6, drastically reduced fluorescence intensity when stored for 48 h, while GQDs dispersed in pH 5.6 shows promising results as the sample was stable for 15 days storage at 25 °C. The GQDs stored in 4 °C showed the excellent stability of fluorescence intensity values as compared to the samples stored at room temperature. The effect at acidic and basic conditions measurably due to the free zigzag sites of the GQDs was protonated, forming a reversible complex between the zigzag sites and H+. Thus the emissive triple carbene state was broken and became inactive in fluorescence in acidic condition, while in alkaline conditions, the free zigzag sites were restored, thereby leading to the restoration of fluorescence [30,31,33].
FTIR was used to characterise for the presence of major functional groups and bonding composition from the optimized freeze dried GQDs sample (Fig. 12 ).

The GQDs exhibit absorption of carboxyl group and a hydroxyl group, showing that nanomaterials contain COOH groups. Additionally, the GQDs show absorption of stretching vibration CH at 2947 cm−1 and stretching vibration of CO in the range of below 1273 cm−1, suggesting that the GQDs contain some incompletely carbonized Citric Acid [34]. A peak related to CO stretching of alkoxy groups at 1136 cm−1. The peaks a 3401 associated with the vibrations of OH. An aromatic CC stretching peak is observed at 1581 cm−1 respectively, represents the complete dehydration of Citric acid to GQDs. The absorption peak at 1028 cm−1 is due to vc-o and peak at 1402 reveals the existence of CH bond. The CH2 rocking vibration emerges from precursor, sometime very weak due to drastic decrease in H concentration during formation of GQDs; intense sharp absorption peak emerges at 823 cm−1 [17].

SEM images clearly shows the uniform sheet structure, but the surface observed from the higher magnification SEM image appears to be rougher and severe aggregation took place and no flake-like structures were observed. This is probably due to storage for a longer time and after freezing temperature, a stacked sheets were aggregated as shown in (Fig. 13 a&b) [31]. Elemental mapping of C and O from GQD was clarified using energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was undertaken. Elemental analysis of synthesized GQD conducted for confirmation of the presence of carbon and oxygen by FESEM/EDX. In the case of GQD, carbon and oxygen were determined from the spectra, in which presence of carbon as well as oxygen was found to be 40.45% and 59.55% respectively (shown in Fig. 13c). From the obtained spectra of the EDX analysis, presence of oxygen was confirmed which is attributed to an oxygen containing functional groups present in GQD. The existence of these elements demonstrates successfully formation of the GQD [8].

The structural information of graphene quantum dots was examined by Raman spectroscopy. The peaks centred at 1355 and 1585 cm−1 are attributed to the D and G bands respectively, of carbon materials. The band at 1355 cm−1 (D) is attributed to a number of defects existing in the carbon framework (sp3), and the band at 1585 cm−1 (G) corresponds to the E2g mode of hexagonal sp2 carbon domains, representing the degree of ripples, edge, and the average size of sp2 domains [31,35] shown in (Fig. 14 ). The ratio of D/G band represents the degree of disordered materials, sp3/sp2 ratio of synthesized GQDs represents the ID/IG ratio which is 0.85, suggest that the GQDs crystalline nature and integrated graphite structure.

Average particle size of synthesized GQDs was determined using Dynamic Light Scattering. Obtained hydrodynamic diameter of particle sounds the stability and redispersibility in aqueous NaOH dispersion medium which is found to be good. From Fig. 15 , the average particle size was found to be 12.7 nm with a polydispserbility index at 0.002. While zeta potential of synthesized GQDs shows found to be – 17.72 mV and conductivity 19.43. Physically solution regains its property and stable for a longer period of time (Fig. 15).

The present work was undertaken in order to synthesize and investigate the Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) from economic sources with scalable methodology. Citric acid was used as a source of carbon material, the effect of electromagnetic waves in presence of vacuum field, it effectively converts to highly fluorescent carbonized product. By varying the precursor concentration, processing parameters such as temperature and pressure, irradiation time, the concentration of dispersion medium, variable pH, the volume of solvent forms the stable highly fluorescent carbonized product in an aqueous environment. Which is then further confirmed by UV and FTIR analysis. Fluorescence and Raman spectroscopic study demonstrate that complete conversion of the citric acid to GQDs. The binding ability of GQDs over the surface by using other metallic nanoparticles shows the new emerging material for bioimaging application and sensing of biomolecules could be possible. Synthesized GQDs are dispersible in aqueous environement and stable at ambient temperature condition can be possible useful for biomedical applications in future. At different environmental conditions in catalysis and bioimaging, the GQDs were stable at different pH conditions. These brief evaluation data helpful in future selection of dispersion medium for researchers working in cell taging, Cell internalization, biomolecules delivery, chemical modification, etc. The stability platform encourage researcher to think on simple techniques intensifications and applications in newer areas.
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to Principal and Management, SES, HRPIPER, Shirpur for providing necessary facilities to carry out research effectively. Authors are also thankful for the kind support from UGC Consortium, Indore for effectively evaluating the synthesized components.
